feedback.text: We appreciate the European Unions ongoing efforts to strengthen digital consumer rights through the proposed Digital Rights Act. In this context, we wish to highlight the importance of addressing the challenges faced by consumers regarding digital game ownership, as underscored by the Stop Killing Games movement. As digital games increasingly depend on online infrastructure and server support, many consumers encounter the frustrating and unfair situation of losing access to games they have legally purchased. The recent permanent shutdown of several online-only games has exposed a significant gap in consumer protection: once purchased, games can become entirely unplayable due to corporate decisions to disable servers or delist titles from digital stores. The Stop Killing Games movement advocates for legal measures ensuring that consumers maintain access to digital games they have paid for, even when publishers choose to discontinue official support. This includes requiring publishers either to keep servers operational or to release server code and essential data to enable community-led preservation and continued playability. We believe incorporating these principles into the Digital Rights Act would substantially enhance consumer protections by: Upholding the fundamental right of consumers to enjoy lasting access to legally purchased digital products. Preventing the arbitrary loss of digital content, which undermines trust in digital marketplaces. Supporting cultural preservation by ensuring video games, as significant digital cultural artifacts, remain accessible for future generations. We recognize concerns raised by industry stakeholders regarding intellectual property and security risks. However, these concerns can be managed through appropriate safeguards, such as timed releases or trusted third-party custodianship, without compromising consumer rights. Furthermore, enabling sustainable digital ownership aligns with the EUs broader goals of fostering innovation, consumer trust, and digital market fairness. The Stop Killing Games movement has already demonstrated considerable public support across the EU, with petitions surpassing one million signatures, reflecting widespread demand for action. We encourage the European Commission to consider the Stop Killing Games movements proposals as part of the Digital Rights Act. Doing so will position the EU as a leader in protecting digital consumers and preserving digital cultural heritage in an increasingly digital economy. Thank you for your attention to this important matter.
